## Claim proved: relative DKEP boundary statement

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\), and let \((M_s,\tau_s)_{s\in V}\) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras. Assume:

1. \(\Gamma\) is irreducible, i.e. \(V\) has no nontrivial join decomposition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) with every vertex of \(V_1\) adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\);
2. for every \(s\in V\), \(M_s\) contains a unitary \(u_s\) with \(\tau_s(u_s)=0\).

Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\ast_\Gamma (M_s,\tau_s)
\]
be the tracial graph product. For every fixed \(v\in V\), set
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Let \(e_B:L^2(M)\to L^2(B)\) be the Jones projection, let \(X_B\) be the DKEP boundary piece generated by \(e_B\), and let \(S_{X_B}(M)\) be the associated DKEP relative operator system. Then:

\[
M=A\ast_B C
\]
trace-preservingly, and \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Hence \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to the boundary piece \(X_B\) for this fixed vertex \(v\). Since \(v\) is arbitrary, this relative conclusion holds for every vertex \(v\).

This is a relative statement with respect to \(X_B\); it is not an assertion of absolute proper proximality.

---

### Proof

Fix \(v\in V\). For \(W\subset V\), write \(M_W\subset M\) for the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_s:s\in W\}\), and write \(E_W:M\to M_W\) for the trace-preserving conditional expectation.

We use the standard graph-product normal-form facts: reduced graph words span a weakly dense \(*\)-subalgebra of \(M\); \(E_W\) kills every reduced graph word containing at least one vertex outside \(W\); for every \(v\),
\[
M\cong M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\ast_{M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}} M_{V\setminus\{v\}};
\]
and
\[
M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\]
state-preservingly, with \(x\otimes y\mapsto xy\). These are the standard graph-product expectation, star tensor, and star-link AFP decomposition results. ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

Thus, with \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\),
\[
M=A\ast_B C,
\]
where \(B=M_L\), \(A=M_{L\cup\{v\}}\), and \(C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}\). Moreover,
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

Let
\[
A^\circ=\ker(E_B|_A),\qquad C^\circ=\ker(E_B|_C),
\]
and set
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The \(L^2\)-space of the amalgamated free product decomposes as
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge1\\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\ne \epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\), and \(P_C\) the projection onto the summands whose first AFP letter is \(C\). Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]
Put
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad R_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

Represent \(M\) on \(L^2(M)\) by left multiplication and write
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J,\qquad x\in M,
\]
for the right action. Let
\[
\mathcal I=\mathcal K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}(M)
\]
be the DKEP \(X_B\)-compact ideal. By the defining DKEP properties, \(e_B\in X_B\subset \mathcal I\), and \(\mathcal I\) is a closed self-adjoint \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule. Hence for every \(x\in M\),
\[
e_B\rho(x),\ \rho(x)e_B\in \mathcal I,
\]
so
\[
[e_B,\rho(x)]\in \mathcal I.
\]

We next show that \(R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M)\). Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\overline D\) denote the other algebra. If \(d\in D^\circ\), then right multiplication by \(d\) preserves both \(R_D L^2(M)\) and its orthogonal complement. Indeed, on an elementary reduced AFP tensor, right multiplication by \(d\) either appends a \(D\)-letter, or, if the last letter is already \(D\), multiplies the last \(D\)-letter and decomposes the result into its \(B\)-part plus centered \(D\)-part. Thus the first AFP letter is unchanged, except that a one-letter \(D\)-word may collapse into \(L^2(B)\), which is still contained in \(R_D L^2(M)\). Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Therefore
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0
\qquad
\forall x\in D.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\overline D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\overline D},\rho(x)]
=
[e_B,\rho(x)]
\in \mathcal I
\qquad
\forall x\in D.
\]
Thus, for \(T=R_A\) or \(T=R_C\),
\[
[T,\rho(x)]\in \mathcal I
\qquad
\forall x\in A\cup C.
\]

Let \(\mathcal A\) be the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\cup C\). Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), \(\mathcal A\) is weakly dense in \(M\). For \(x,y\in\mathcal A\),
\[
\rho(xy)=\rho(y)\rho(x),
\]
and
\[
[T,\rho(xy)]
=
[T,\rho(y)]\rho(x)+\rho(y)[T,\rho(x)].
\]
Because \(\mathcal I\) is a \(JMJ\)-bimodule, induction gives
\[
[T,\rho(z)]\in\mathcal I
\qquad
\forall z\in\mathcal A.
\]

Now take \(z\in M\). By Kaplansky density, choose uniformly bounded \(z_i\in\mathcal A\) with \(z_i\to z\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). For \(y\in M\),
\[
\|[T,\rho(y)]\|_{\infty,1}
\le 2\|T\|\,\|y\|_2.
\]
Indeed, for \(\|m\|_\infty\le1\),
\[
[T,\rho(y)]\widehat m
=
T\widehat{my}-(T\widehat m)y,
\]
so by Cauchy–Schwarz in noncommutative \(L^p\)-spaces,
\[
\|T\widehat{my}\|_1\le \|T\|\,\|my\|_2\le \|T\|\,\|y\|_2,
\]
and
\[
\|(T\widehat m)y\|_1
\le \|T\widehat m\|_2\,\|y\|_2
\le \|T\|\,\|y\|_2.
\]
Therefore \([T,\rho(z_i)]\to [T,\rho(z)]\) in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\). Since \(\mathcal I\) is closed, \([T,\rho(z)]\in\mathcal I\). Hence
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Also \(e_B\in S_{X_B}(M)\), and therefore
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

We now choose suitable unitaries. Since
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) and define
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0,
\]
so \(a\in A^\circ\).

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\). Irreducibility implies \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\): if \(N(v)=\varnothing\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(V\setminus\{v\}\), so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
is a nontrivial join decomposition.

We claim that there exist unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C.
\]
Since \(w_i\notin L\), the reduced graph word \(u_{w_i}\) contains a vertex outside \(L\), so \(E_B(c_i)=0\). For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) is a reduced graph word containing vertices outside \(L\), hence
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0.
\]
Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=E_B(1)=1\).

It remains to handle the case \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge3\), the link \(L\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition: every vertex in \(\{v,w\}\) would be adjacent to every vertex in \(L\). Therefore there exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\), and set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Both \(c_1,c_2\) are unitaries in \(C\). The words \(u_w\) and \(u_z u_w\) are reduced graph words containing \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1)=E_B(c_2)=0.
\]
Also,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\notin \operatorname{Star}(w)\), the word \((w,z,w)\) is reduced. It contains \(w\notin L\), hence
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and of course \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Thus the desired \(c_1,c_2\) exist.

Now set
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus P_A L^2(M),
\qquad
H_C^+=R_C L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus P_C L^2(M).
\]
If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it prepends a centered \(C\)-letter to every reduced AFP tensor beginning with an \(A\)-letter. Therefore
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C
\qquad
(i=1,2).
\]
Moreover, since \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), we have \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\). Thus, for \(\xi,\eta\in H_A^+\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,(c_1^*c_2)\eta\rangle
=
0,
\]
because \((c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M)\), which is orthogonal to \(H_A^+\). Hence the ranges \(c_1H_A^+\) and \(c_2H_A^+\) are orthogonal, and
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]
Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_A L^2(M)\), so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

Assume, for contradiction, that \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Thus
\[
\omega(xT)=\omega(Tx)
\qquad
\forall x\in M,\ T\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Since \(S_{X_B}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule, centrality implies unitary invariance:
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

Applying \(\omega\) to
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C
\]
gives
\[
2\omega(R_A)\le \omega(P_C).
\]
Applying \(\omega\) to
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A
\]
gives
\[
\omega(R_C)\le \omega(P_A).
\]

Let
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),\qquad \beta=\omega(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0,
\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Because
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad R_C=e_B+P_C,
\]
the two inequalities become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\]
and
\[
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining them,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge0\), this forces \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma\le0\), so \(\gamma=0\). This contradicts
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state.

By the DKEP definition, \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\) if there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\) whose restriction to the left copy of \(M\) is normal. We proved the stronger statement that no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\) exists at all. Thus \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\) for the fixed vertex \(v\). Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, the same conclusion holds for every vertex. ∎
problem_solved: After the routine fixes above, the proof establishes the relative statement: for every vertex \(v\), with \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\), \(A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}\), and \(C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}\), one has the trace-preserving AFP decomposition \(M=A*_B C\), and the DKEP operator system \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Consequently, the inclusion \(M\subset M\) is properly proximal relative to the boundary piece \(X_B\) for every vertex \(v\). It does not prove absolute proper proximality of \(M\) with respect to the compact boundary \(K(L^2M)\).

## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

This write-up proves the following **abstract DKEP finite-cover upgrade lemma**, not the full graph-product theorem stated above. Thus it is a relaxation/different result: it assumes the relevant DKEP boundary pieces, support projections, support sandwich inequality, and relative proper proximality as hypotheses, and proves only the final upgrade from relative proper proximality for finitely many pieces to absolute proper proximality.

Let \((M,\tau)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra, let \(H=L^2(M,\tau)\), and let \(J\) be the modular conjugation, so that \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\) denotes the right action of \(M\) on \(H\). Assume \(M\) is placed in the DKEP properly proximal framework: there is a unital von Neumann algebra \(\mathcal N\), called the normal-bidual compactification, containing the left copy of \(M\) and containing support projections for DKEP boundary pieces. A DKEP boundary piece \(X\subset B(H)\) means a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra, invariant under the left and right \(M\)-actions, whose elements are treated as \(X\)-compact operators. Its DKEP support projection \(q_X\in\mathcal N\) is the projection obtained as the weak-* limit in \(\mathcal N\) of any contractive approximate identity of \(X\), equivalently the least projection supporting the weak-* closure of \(X\). The compact support \(q_K\in\mathcal N\) is the support projection of \(K(H)\).

Let \(n\ge 1\), let \(U_1,\dots,U_n\) be sets with finite-cover condition
\[
\bigcap_{i=1}^n U_i=\varnothing,
\]
and let
\[
\mathcal F=\left\{\bigcap_{i\in I}U_i:\varnothing\ne I\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}\right\}\cup\{\varnothing\}.
\]
For every \(U\in\mathcal F\), suppose there is a DKEP boundary piece \(X_U\) with support projection \(q_U\in\mathcal N\). Assume \(q_{\varnothing}=q_K\). Assume also the support sandwich inequality
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}
\]
in the positive order of \(\mathcal N\), for all \(U,W\in\mathcal F\).

For a boundary piece \(X\), define
\[
S_X(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in X\text{ for every }x\in M\}.
\]
A state \(\psi\) on \(S_X(M)\) is \(M\)-central if \(\psi(xT)=\psi(Tx)\) for all \(x\in M\), \(T\in S_X(M)\). The algebra \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) if there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to the left copy of \(M\) is normal. The algebra \(M\) has no amenable direct summand if there is no nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable.

Use the following DKEP obstruction theorem as a black box: if \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central obstruction state \(\Omega\) on \(\mathcal N\), normal on \(M\), such that \(\Omega(q_K)=0\); moreover, if \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\), then every such obstruction state satisfies \(\Omega(q_U)=1\).

Under these assumptions, if \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is properly proximal relative to each \(X_{U_i}\), then \(M\) is properly proximal.

## Proof

All products and inequalities below are taken inside the normal-bidual compactification \(\mathcal N\). We deliberately do **not** assume that the projections \(q_U\) commute.

### 1. A state-support fact

Let \(A\) be a unital \(C^*\)-algebra, let \(\phi\) be a state on \(A\), and let \(p\in A\) be a projection with \(\phi(p)=1\). Then
\[
\phi(a)=\phi(pap)
\qquad\text{for every }a\in A.
\]

Indeed, let \(e=1-p\). Then \(e\) is a projection and
\[
\phi(e)=\phi(1)-\phi(p)=0.
\]
By Cauchy–Schwarz for the positive functional \(\phi\),
\[
|\phi(ea)|^2\le \phi(e^*e)\phi(a^*a)=\phi(e)\phi(a^*a)=0,
\]
so \(\phi(ea)=0\) for all \(a\). Similarly,
\[
\phi(ae)=\overline{\phi(ea^*)}=0.
\]
Decomposing
\[
a=pap+pae+eap+eae,
\]
and applying \(\phi\), all terms except \(pap\) vanish. Hence \(\phi(a)=\phi(pap)\).

### 2. Two support-one projections force support one on the intersection

Let \(\phi\) be a state on \(\mathcal N\). Suppose \(U,W\in\mathcal F\) and
\[
\phi(q_U)=1,\qquad \phi(q_W)=1.
\]
Set
\[
p=q_U,\qquad s=q_W,\qquad r=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
By the state-support fact applied to \(p\) and \(a=s\),
\[
\phi(s)=\phi(psp).
\]
Since \(\phi(s)=1\), we have
\[
\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=1.
\]
By the support sandwich inequality,
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}=r.
\]
Because \(r\) is a projection, \(0\le r\le 1\), so
\[
1=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)\le \phi(r)\le 1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

No commutativity was used: \(q_Uq_Wq_U\) is positive because
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U=(q_Wq_U)^*(q_Wq_U),
\]
and the argument uses only the compression \(q_Uq_Wq_U\), not the product \(q_Uq_W\).

### 3. Apply the obstruction theorem

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central obstruction state \(\Omega\) on \(\mathcal N\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

For each \(i=1,\dots,n\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{U_i}\). Hence the second part of the obstruction theorem gives
\[
\Omega(q_{U_i})=1
\qquad\text{for every }i=1,\dots,n.
\]

### 4. Iterate the two-support argument

Define
\[
V_k=U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_k
\qquad (1\le k\le n).
\]
We prove by induction that
\[
\Omega(q_{V_k})=1
\qquad\text{for every }k.
\]

For \(k=1\), this is exactly \(\Omega(q_{U_1})=1\).

Assume \(\Omega(q_{V_k})=1\). Since \(\Omega(q_{U_{k+1}})=1\), the result of Section 2, applied with \(U=V_k\) and \(W=U_{k+1}\), gives
\[
\Omega(q_{V_k\cap U_{k+1}})=1.
\]
But
\[
V_k\cap U_{k+1}=V_{k+1}.
\]
Thus
\[
\Omega(q_{V_{k+1}})=1.
\]
By induction,
\[
\Omega(q_{V_n})=1.
\]

The finite-cover condition says
\[
V_n=U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Omega(q_{\varnothing})=1.
\]
By hypothesis,
\[
q_{\varnothing}=q_K.
\]
Hence
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1.
\]

This contradicts the obstruction-state condition \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). Therefore the assumption that \(M\) is not properly proximal is false. Consequently, \(M\) is properly proximal.
problem_solved: The proof establishes the abstract finite-cover upgrade lemma: assuming a separable tracial von Neumann algebra \(M\) lies in the stated DKEP framework, has no amenable direct summand, has boundary-piece support projections \(q_U\) for all finite intersections with \(q_\varnothing=q_K\), satisfies \(q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}\), and satisfies the stated DKEP obstruction theorem, then relative proper proximality of \(M\) with respect to each \(X_{U_i}\) and \(\bigcap_i U_i=\varnothing\) imply that \(M\) is properly proximal.

**Claim (relative link proper proximality).**  
Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\), and assume that \(\Gamma\) is irreducible, meaning that it is not a nontrivial join of two nonempty induced subgraphs. Let \((M_s,\tau_s)_{s\in V}\) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, and assume that each \(M_s\) contains a unitary \(u_s\) with \(\tau_s(u_s)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\ast_{\Gamma,s\in V}(M_s,\tau_s)
\]
be the tracial graph product.

For every \(v\in V\), put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L\subset M.
\]
Let \(e_B:L^2(M)\to L^2(B)\) be the Jones projection and let \(X_B\) be the DKEP Jones-projection boundary piece associated with \(B\). Then the DKEP operator system \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Consequently \(M\) is properly proximal relative to the link boundary piece \(X_B=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\).

---

Fix \(v\in V\). Write \(H=L^2(M)\), identify \(M\) with its left regular representation on \(H\), and write
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J,\qquad x\in M,
\]
so that \(\rho(x)\) is right multiplication by \(x\).

For a trace-preserving von Neumann subalgebra \(B\subset M\), the DKEP Jones-projection boundary piece is
\[
X_B
=
\overline{\operatorname{span}}^{\|\cdot\|}
\{\,x_1\rho(y_1)T\rho(y_2)x_2:
x_i,y_i\in M,\ T\in e_BB(H)e_B\,\}.
\]
It is an \(M\)-boundary piece: \(M\) and \(\rho(M)\) lie in its multiplier algebra, \(e_B\in X_B\), and \(K(H)\subset X_B\). Indeed, since \(\Omega=\widehat 1\in L^2(B)\), the rank-one projection \(p_\Omega\) belongs to \(e_BB(H)e_B\). For \(x,y\in M\), the operators \(x p_\Omega\rho(y^*)\) are rank-one operators between vectors in the dense subspace \(M\Omega\subset H\), so their span is norm-dense in \(K(H)\).

Let \(\mathcal K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}(M)\) denote the DKEP \(X_B\)-compact ideal. We use the standard DKEP facts that \(X_B\subset \mathcal K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}(M)\), that \(\mathcal K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}(M)\) is an \(M\)-\(\rho(M)\)-bimodule, and that
\[
S_{X_B}(M)
=
\{\,T\in B(H): [T,\rho(x)]\in \mathcal K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}(M)\ \forall x\in M\,\}
\]
is an \(M\)-bimodular operator system containing \(M\) and \(X_B\). We also use the standard generator criterion: to verify \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\), it is enough to check the commutator condition on von Neumann subalgebras that generate \(M\).

Set
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}=M_{L\cup\{v\}},
\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) commutes precisely with the vertices in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B.
\]
By the star-link decomposition for graph products,
\[
M=A\ast_B C
\]
as a tracial amalgamated free product.

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
Then
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge 1\\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\ne \epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) together with all reduced summands whose first letter is \(A\), and define \(R_C\) analogously. Put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,
\qquad
P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C
\]
is an orthogonal decomposition.

We first show that
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\overline D\) denote the other algebra. Put
\[
D^\circ=\ker(E_B|_D).
\]
Right multiplication by an element of \(D^\circ\) preserves \(R_DH\). Indeed, on the dense subspace of reduced AFP tensors, multiplying on the right by \(x\in D^\circ\) either appends a final \(D\)-letter, or modifies the final \(D\)-letter and possibly produces a \(B\)-component; in all cases the first letter remains \(D\), except that a one-letter \(D\)-word may collapse into \(L^2(B)\), which is still contained in \(R_DH\). The same applies to \(x^*\). Hence
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0,\qquad x\in D^\circ.
\]
Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector, so \(R_D\) also commutes with \(\rho(B)\). Therefore \(R_D\) commutes with \(\rho(D)\).

Since
\[
R_{\overline D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
for \(x\in D^\circ\) we get
\[
[R_{\overline D},\rho(x)]
=
[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
Because \(e_B\in X_B\) and \(\rho(M)\) lies in the multiplier algebra of \(X_B\),
\[
[e_B,\rho(x)]\in X_B\subset \mathcal K^{\infty,1}_{X_B}(M).
\]
Thus the commutator condition for both \(R_A\) and \(R_C\) holds on \(A\) and on \(C\). Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Also \(e_B\in X_B\subset S_{X_B}(M)\), hence
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

Now choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Under \(A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B\), set
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0.
\]

We next construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1_B.
\]
Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\). Since \(\Gamma\) is irreducible, \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\); otherwise \(\Gamma\) would be the join of \(\{v\}\) and \(V\setminus\{v\}\).

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1_B\). For \(i\neq j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) is graph-reduced and contains centered syllables from vertices outside \(L\), so the graph-product conditional expectation onto \(B=M_L\) kills it. Thus
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1_B.
\]

It remains to treat the case \(|N(v)|=1\). Write \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), the link \(L\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every vertex of \(L\), then \(\Gamma\) would be the join of the induced subgraphs on \(\{v,w\}\) and on \(L\), contradicting irreducibility. Hence there exists \(z\in L\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\). Choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\). Define
\[
c_1=u_w,
\qquad
c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(u_z\in B\) and \(w\notin L\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1_B\). Finally,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), this is a graph-reduced word containing centered \(w\)-syllables outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Hence again
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1_B.
\]

We now use these unitaries to obtain projection inequalities. If \(d\in C\) satisfies \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(R_AH\) into \(P_CH\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it sends every reduced tensor beginning with \(A\) to a reduced tensor beginning with \(C\). Therefore, for \(i=1,2\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]
Moreover, the two ranges are orthogonal. Indeed, for \(\xi,\eta\in R_AH\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(c_1^*c_2\in C\) and \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), left multiplication by \(c_1^*c_2\) sends \(R_AH\) into \(P_CH\), which is orthogonal to \(R_AH\). Hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]
Similarly, since \(a\in A\) and \(E_B(a)=0\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(R_CH\) into \(P_AH\), so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

Let
\[
S=S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Suppose, toward a contradiction, that \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S\), meaning
\[
\omega(xT)=\omega(Tx),
\qquad x\in M,\ T\in S.
\]
Since \(S\) is \(M\)-bimodular, for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S\),
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T).
\]
Applying this to the projection inequalities above gives
\[
2\omega(R_A)\le \omega(P_C),
\qquad
\omega(R_C)\le \omega(P_A).
\]
Set
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),
\qquad
\beta=\omega(P_A),
\qquad
\gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Because \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,
\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,
\qquad
R_C=e_B+P_C.
\]
Thus
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining these inequalities,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma
\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)
=
3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\), we get \(\alpha=\beta=0\). The inequality \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) then gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Therefore \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state.

Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, the same conclusion holds for every link boundary piece \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Thus \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\).
problem_solved: After the routine citation and normal-form patches above, the proof establishes the following relative statement: under the stated graph-product hypotheses, for every vertex \(v\), with \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) and \(X_B\) the DKEP Jones-projection boundary piece associated to \(B\), the operator system \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\). The proof does not establish absolute DKEP proper proximality relative to \(K(L^2M)\).

Final_Solution:
## Claim (link-relative form)

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\), irreducible in the sense that \(\Gamma\) is not a non-trivial join. Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, and assume that each \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra.

For every vertex \(v\in V\), writing \(\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), the DKEP operator system \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. Hence \(M\) is properly proximal relative to the DKEP boundary piece \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every \(v\in V\). Moreover, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 1. DKEP boundary notation

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let
\[
e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the Jones projection.

Put \(H=L^2(M)\). For \(a,b\in M\), let
\[
L_a\widehat x=\widehat{ax},
\qquad
\rho_b\widehat x=\widehat{xb}.
\]
Thus \(\rho_b=Jb^*J\).

The DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\) is
\[
X_U
=
\overline{\operatorname{span}}^{\|\cdot\|}
\{sTt^*:s,t\in \mathcal R_U,\ T\in B(H)\},
\]
where
\[
\mathcal R_U=\{L_a\rho_b e_U:a,b\in M\}.
\]
Let \(K_U=K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\) be the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed ideal generated by \(X_U\), and define
\[
S_{X_U}(M)
=
\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho_x]\in K_U\ \text{for every }x\in M\}.
\]
A state \(\phi\) on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is \(M\)-central if
\[
\phi(L_xT)=\phi(TL_x)
\qquad
(x\in M,\ T\in S_{X_U}(M)).
\]

We use the standard DKEP facts that \(K_U\) is a self-adjoint strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule, and that for fixed \(T\in B(H)\), the set
\[
\mathcal N_T
=
\{x\in M:[T,\rho_x],[T,\rho_{x^*}]\in K_U\}
\]
is a von Neumann subalgebra of \(M\). Consequently, to prove \(T\in S_{X_U}(M)\), it suffices to check the commutator condition on von Neumann subalgebras generating \(M\).

Also, \(S_{X_U}(M)\) is stable under left multiplication by \(M\) on either side: if \(T\in S_{X_U}(M)\) and \(y\in M\), then
\[
[L_yT,\rho_x]=L_y[T,\rho_x]\in K_U,
\]
and
\[
[TL_y,\rho_x]=[T,\rho_x]L_y\in K_U.
\]

Finally, for every \(x\in M\),
\[
\rho_xe_U,\ e_U\rho_x\in X_U.
\]
Indeed, \(\rho_xe_U=L_1\rho_xe_U\in \mathcal R_U\), and since \(e_U\in \mathcal R_U\),
\[
\rho_xe_U=(\rho_xe_U)1e_U^*\in X_U.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(e_U\rho_x\in X_U\). Hence
\[
[e_U,\rho_x]\in X_U\subset K_U,
\]
so \(e_U\in S_{X_U}(M)\).

---

### 2. The star-link AFP decomposition

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v),
\qquad
B=M_\Lambda,
\]
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}=M_{\{v\}\cup\Lambda},
\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(\Lambda\), the graph-product relations give
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\]
and
\[
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

By the standard graph-product normal form, equivalently the Caspers-Fima star-link decomposition,
\[
M=A*_B C
\]
with respect to the trace-preserving conditional expectations onto \(B\).

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP Hilbert-space decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge 1\\ \epsilon_1,\dots,\epsilon_n\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\ne \epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the direct sum of reduced AFP sectors whose first letter is \(A\), and define \(P_C\) similarly. Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C,
\]
where \(e_B=e_\Lambda\). Set
\[
F_A=e_B+P_A,
\qquad
F_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

We claim that
\[
F_A,F_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\bar D\) denote the other algebra. Write
\[
D^\circ=\ker(E_B|_D).
\]
Take \(d\in D\), and decompose \(d=E_B(d)+d^\circ\) with \(d^\circ\in D^\circ\). Right multiplication by \(E_B(d)\in B\) preserves every AFP sector.

On the dense algebraic reduced tensor space, right multiplication by \(d^\circ\) behaves as follows. If a reduced tensor ends in the algebra \(\bar D\), right multiplication by \(d^\circ\) appends a new \(D\)-letter. If it already ends in \(D\), the product of the last \(D\)-letter with \(d^\circ\) decomposes into its \(B\)-expectation plus its centered \(D^\circ\)-part; the \(B\)-part collapses the last tensor, while the centered part remains in a \(D\)-sector. Thus right multiplication by \(d^\circ\) never changes the first letter, except that a one-letter \(D\)-sector may collapse into \(L^2(B)\). Also, \(L^2(B)\) is sent into \(K_D\).

Therefore both \(F_DH\) and its orthogonal complement \(P_{\bar D}H\) are invariant under \(\rho(D)\). Hence
\[
[F_D,\rho_x]=0
\qquad
(x\in D).
\]
For \(x\in \bar D\), using
\[
F_D=1-F_{\bar D}+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[F_D,\rho_x]=[e_B,\rho_x]\in X_\Lambda\subset K_\Lambda,
\]
because \([F_{\bar D},\rho_x]=0\).

Thus the commutator condition holds on both generating subalgebras \(A\) and \(C\). Since \(M=W^*(A,C)\), the DKEP generating-set criterion gives
\[
F_D\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\), it follows that
\[
P_D=F_D-e_B\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]
Hence
\[
F_A,F_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M).
\]

---

### 3. Choosing the ping-pong unitaries

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of vertices not adjacent to \(v\).

Irreducibility implies \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\). Indeed, if \(N(v)=\varnothing\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(V\setminus\{v\}\), so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a non-trivial join decomposition.

First choose a unitary in \(A^\circ=\ker(E_B|_A)\). Under the identification
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
the unitary
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B
\]
satisfies
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0.
\]
Thus
\[
a\in A^\circ.
\]

We next choose two \(B\)-orthogonal unitaries in \(C^\circ=\ker(E_B|_C)\).

Use the standard graph-product expectation fact: \(E_B\) kills every reduced graph-product word containing at least one vertex outside \(\Lambda\).

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\subset C,
\qquad i=1,2.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C^\circ\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Now suppose \(|N(v)|=1\), say \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), the link \(\Lambda\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every vertex in \(\Lambda\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \Lambda
\]
would be a non-trivial join decomposition: \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(\Lambda\) by definition of \(\Lambda\), and \(w\) would be adjacent to every vertex of \(\Lambda\) by assumption. Hence there exists \(z\in\Lambda\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

Define
\[
c_1=u_w,
\qquad
c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Both are unitaries in \(C\). Also,
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_2)=E_B(u_z u_w)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0,
\]
so \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\). Moreover,
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1
\qquad
(i=1,2),
\]
and
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced and contains the vertex \(w\notin\Lambda\). Therefore
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Hence again
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Thus in all cases we have unitaries
\[
a\in A^\circ,
\qquad
c_1,c_2\in C^\circ,
\]
such that
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

---

### 4. AFP ping-pong estimates

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\). The reduced-tensor calculation also gives the following left-multiplication inclusion:
\[
L_{d}F_{\bar D}H\subset P_DH
\qquad
(d\in D^\circ).
\]
Indeed, if \(d\in D^\circ\), then \(dL^2(B)\subset K_D\), and if a reduced sector has first letter \(\bar D\), left multiplication by \(d\) prepends a \(D\)-letter, producing a reduced sector with first letter \(D\).

Applying this with \(D=C\) gives
\[
L_{c_i}F_AH\subset P_CH,
\qquad i=1,2.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
L_{c_i}F_A L_{c_i}^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying it with \(D=A\) gives
\[
L_aF_CH\subset P_AH,
\]
hence
\[
L_aF_C L_a^*\le P_A.
\]

We also need orthogonality of the two \(C\)-translates of \(F_AH\). For \(\xi,\eta\in F_AH\),
\[
\langle L_{c_1}\xi,L_{c_2}\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,L_{c_1^*c_2}\eta\rangle.
\]
Since
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0,
\]
we have \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\). Therefore
\[
L_{c_1^*c_2}\eta\in P_CH.
\]
But
\[
F_AH=L^2(B)\oplus P_AH
\]
is orthogonal to \(P_CH\). Hence
\[
\langle L_{c_1}\xi,L_{c_2}\eta\rangle=0.
\]
Thus the projections
\[
L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*
\quad\text{and}\quad
L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*
\]
are orthogonal, and
\[
L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*
+
L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*
\le P_C.
\]

---

### 5. Absence of \(M\)-central states on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\)

Suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\).

Because \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\) is stable under left multiplication by \(M\), for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\),
\[
\phi(L_uTL_u^*)=\phi(T).
\]
Indeed, \(L_uT\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\), and by \(M\)-centrality,
\[
\phi(T)
=
\phi(L_{u^*}L_uT)
=
\phi(L_uTL_{u^*})
=
\phi(L_uTL_u^*).
\]

Apply this to \(u=c_1,c_2\) and \(T=F_A\). Since the two translates are orthogonal and dominated by \(P_C\),
\[
2\phi(F_A)
=
\phi(L_{c_1}F_A L_{c_1}^*)
+
\phi(L_{c_2}F_A L_{c_2}^*)
\le
\phi(P_C).
\]
Apply the same argument to \(u=a\) and \(T=F_C\). Since
\[
L_aF_C L_a^*\le P_A,
\]
we get
\[
\phi(F_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),
\qquad
\beta=\phi(P_A),
\qquad
\gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Because \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are pairwise orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,
\qquad
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also,
\[
\phi(F_A)=\alpha+\beta,
\qquad
\phi(F_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
The inequalities above become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining them,
\[
\beta
\ge
\alpha+\gamma
\ge
\alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)
=
3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Thus
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\), we get
\[
\alpha=\beta=0.
\]
Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\). Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, this holds for every \(\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every vertex \(v\).

---

### 6. No amenable direct summand

Suppose, toward contradiction, that \(M\) has a nonzero amenable direct summand. Then there is a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable.

Let
\[
p=L_z
\]
be the projection from \(L^2(M)\) onto \(L^2(zM)\). Equip \(zM\) with the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(zx)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]

Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, equivalently injective, there exists a conditional expectation
\[
\Theta:B(L^2(zM,\tau_z))\to zM.
\]
Define
\[
\psi=\tau_z\circ\Theta.
\]
By Tomiyama bimodularity,
\[
\Theta(yT)=y\Theta(T),
\qquad
\Theta(Ty)=\Theta(T)y
\]
for \(y\in zM\). Since \(\tau_z\) is tracial,
\[
\psi(yT)=\psi(Ty)
\qquad
(y\in zM,\ T\in B(L^2(zM))).
\]
Thus \(\psi\) is a \(zM\)-central hypertrace, and
\[
\psi|_{zM}=\tau_z.
\]

Fix any vertex \(v\in V\), and put \(\Lambda=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). Define a state \(\Phi\) on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\) by
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(pTp|_{pL^2(M)}).
\]
Since \(z\) is central, \(p=L_z\) commutes with every \(L_x\), \(x\in M\). For \(x\in M\) and \(T\in S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\),
\[
pL_xTp=(pL_xp)(pTp),
\]
and
\[
pTL_xp=(pTp)(pL_xp).
\]
Moreover,
\[
pL_xp=L_{zx}|_{pL^2(M)},
\]
which is left multiplication by an element of \(zM\). Therefore, using the \(zM\)-centrality of \(\psi\),
\[
\Phi(L_xT)
=
\psi((pL_xp)(pTp))
=
\psi((pTp)(pL_xp))
=
\Phi(TL_x).
\]
Hence \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\).

Its restriction to the left copy of \(M\) is
\[
\Phi(L_x)
=
\psi(pL_xp)
=
\psi(L_{zx})
=
\tau_z(zx)
=
\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]
This is a normal state on \(M\). This contradicts the absence of \(M\)-central states on \(S_{X_\Lambda}(M)\). Therefore \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand.

Final_Solution:
## Theorem

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). Assume that \(\Gamma\) is **join-irreducible**, i.e. there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into two nonempty sets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\ast_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)_{v\in V}
\]
be the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra. Then \(M\) is properly proximal.

Here proper proximality is in the sense of Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson.

---

### 1. Normal-bidual tools

We use the following standard normal-bidual package for small-at-infinity compactifications of tracial von Neumann algebras. For a tracial von Neumann algebra \(N\), write \(H=L^2(N)\), let \(J\) be the tracial modular conjugation, and identify the left and right copies of \(N\) in \(B(H)\) by
\[
L_x\widehat y=\widehat{xy},\qquad R_x\widehat y=\widehat{yx}=Jx^*J\widehat y .
\]

If \(X\subset B(H)\) is an \(N\)-boundary piece, let \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) be the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed compact ideal, where
\[
\|T\|_{\infty,1}
=
\sup_{a,b\in (N)_1}\left|\langle T\widehat a,\widehat b\rangle\right|.
\]
The relative small-at-infinity operator system is
\[
S_X(N)=\{T\in B(L^2N):[T,R_x]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\ \forall x\in N\}.
\]
When \(X=K(L^2N)\), write \(S(N)\). Proper proximality relative to \(X\) means that there is no \(N\)-central state on \(S_X(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is normal. The absolute case is \(X=K(L^2N)\). These definitions and the equivalent state formulations are those of DKEP, especially their definition of \(S_X(N)\) and Theorem 6.2. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

We also use the normal-bidual compactification. Let
\[
\widetilde S_X(N)=(S_X(N)^\#_J)^*
\]
denote the normal bidual, where the \(\#_J\)-dual imposes normality for both the left \(N\)- and right \(JNJ\)-module structures. Let
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}:B(L^2N)\to (B(L^2N)^\#_J)^*
\]
be the canonical normal-bidual u.c.p. map. For each boundary piece \(X\), there is a support projection \(q_X\) in the normal bidual, namely the identity of \((K_X(N)^\#_J)^*\). This projection commutes with the normal copies of \(N\) and \(JNJ\). If \(0\le T\le 1\) and \(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), then
\[
0\le \iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)\le q_X .
\]
The normal-bidual construction, the projection \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\), and support projections for hereditary subalgebras are recorded in DKEP §2 and Ding--Peterson §3.1. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

We shall use the following consequences.

1. **Generator criterion.** If \(\mathcal G\subset N\) is a self-adjoint von Neumann generating set and \(T\in B(L^2N)\) satisfies
   \[
   [T,R_g]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\qquad(g\in\mathcal G),
   \]
   then \(T\in S_X(N)\).

2. **Jones multiplicative domain.** If \(B_1,\dots,B_k\subset N\) are von Neumann subalgebras and \(e_{B_i}:L^2N\to L^2B_i\) are the Jones projections, then \(\iota(e_{B_i})\) commutes with \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\). Hence \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is multiplicative on
   \[
   C^*(N,JNJ,e_{B_1},\dots,e_{B_k}).
   \]
   This is the Jones-projection normality/multiplicative-domain consequence of the normal-bidual formalism; Ding--Peterson Corollary 3.8 gives the needed continuity for Jones compression. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.10161))

3. **Approximate-unit convergence.** If \(D\subset K_X(N)\) is a \(C^*\)-subalgebra whose image is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), and if \((h_i)\subset D\) is a positive contractive approximate unit lying in a Jones-generated multiplicative domain as above, then
   \[
   \iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_i)\xrightarrow{\ \sigma\text{-weak}\ }q_X .
   \]

4. **Compression principle.** If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), and if \(\Omega\) is an \(N\)-central state on the absolute normal-bidual compactification \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\), then
   \[
   \Omega(q_X)=1.
   \]
   Indeed, if \(\Omega(q_X^\perp)>0\), then
   \[
   \Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(q_X^\perp Tq_X^\perp)}{\Omega(q_X^\perp)},\qquad T\in \widetilde S_X(N),
   \]
   is \(N\)-central because \(q_X\) commutes with \(N\) and \(JNJ\). For \(x\ge0\) in \(N\),
   \[
   0\le \Omega(xq_X^\perp)\le \Omega(x),
   \]
   so \(\Phi|_N\) is normal by domination. The inclusion
   \[
   q_X^\perp\widetilde S_X(N)q_X^\perp\subset q_K^\perp\widetilde S(N)
   \]
   identifies this compression with a state on the relative compactification, contradicting relative proper proximality.

5. **Obstruction state.** If \(N\) is separable, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there is an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0,
   \]
   where \(q_K\) is the support projection of \(K(L^2N)\). This is the normal-bidual form of DKEP Proposition 8.2: if the obstruction state had positive mass on \(q_K\), compression to the compact corner would give an \(N\)-central hypertrace on a nonzero central summand, hence an amenable direct summand. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

---

### 2. Graph-product notation and finite propagation

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph product. Thus \(\mathcal A\) is the linear span of \(1\) and reduced Wick words
\[
x_1\cdots x_n,\qquad x_i\in M_{v_i}^\circ:=\ker\tau_{v_i},
\]
where \((v_1,\dots,v_n)\) is reduced in the graph-product sense: no two equal vertices can be brought next to each other by commuting adjacent syllables from adjacent vertices.

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the von Neumann algebra generated by \(\{M_v:v\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation and \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) the Jones projection. On reduced Wick words,
\[
E_U(x_1\cdots x_n)=
\begin{cases}
x_1\cdots x_n,& v_i\in U\ \text{for every }i,\\
0,&\text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
\]
In particular,
\[
E_U E_W=E_{U\cap W},\qquad e_Ue_W=e_{U\cap W}.
\]

For \(U\subset V\), write
\[
X_U:=X_{M_U},
\qquad
q_U:=q_{X_U}.
\]

We need a finite-propagation lemma for graph-product normal forms.

#### Lemma 2.1: finite propagation through intersections

Let \(U,W\subset V\), put \(I=U\cap W\), and let \(a,b\in\mathcal A\). Then there are finitely many tuples
\[
(\alpha_r,\beta_r,\gamma_r,\delta_r,J_r),
\qquad
\alpha_r,\beta_r,\gamma_r,\delta_r\in\mathcal A,\quad J_r\subset I,
\]
such that for every \(y\in\mathcal A_W\),
\[
E_U(ayb)=
\sum_r \alpha_r\,E_{J_r}(\gamma_r y\delta_r)\,\beta_r .
\]
Consequently,
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\sum_r
L_{\alpha_r}R_{\beta_r}\,e_{J_r}\,L_{\gamma_r}R_{\delta_r}\,e_W .
\]
The adjoint form also holds:
\[
e_W L_aR_b e_U
\in
\operatorname{span}
\{e_W L_\alpha R_\beta e_J L_\gamma R_\delta:
\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A,\ J\subset I\}.
\]

**Proof.** By linearity it is enough to take \(a\) and \(b\) reduced Wick words. Fix a total order on \(V\), and use Green’s normal form: every shuffle class of reduced vertex words has a unique representative, obtained by commuting adjacent syllables from adjacent vertices until the chosen ordered representative is reached.

We recall the elementary multiplication rule. Let \(x\in M_s^\circ\) and let
\[
\eta=x_1\cdots x_n,\qquad x_i\in M_{v_i}^\circ,
\]
be a reduced Wick word in Green normal form. To reduce \(x\eta\), one searches for the first syllable \(x_k\) with \(v_k=s\) such that all preceding vertices \(v_1,\dots,v_{k-1}\) are adjacent to \(s\). If no such \(k\) exists, \(x\) is inserted into the unique Green position among the initial syllables commuting with \(s\), giving one reduced word. If such \(k\) exists, \(x\) is shuffled next to \(x_k\), and the product inside \(M_s\) is expanded as
\[
xx_k=\tau_s(xx_k)1+(xx_k)^\circ .
\]
Thus there are at most two output terms: a scalar branch deleting the \(s\)-syllable and a centered branch replacing it by \((xx_k)^\circ\). The analogous rule holds for right multiplication.

Now reduce \(ayb\), where \(y\in\mathcal A_W\), by applying this elementary rule to the fixed syllables of \(a\) and \(b\). Only the syllables belonging to the fixed words \(a\) and \(b\) can initiate reductions with syllables of \(y\). Hence the reduction process has only finitely many possible diagrams: a diagram records, for each fixed syllable, whether it survives, is absorbed into a scalar contraction, or remains as a fixed multiplier inside the conditional expectation. The branching is finite because each fixed syllable gives at most two local branches.

Consider one terminal diagram contributing nontrivially to \(E_U(ayb)\). Any syllable of \(y\) that is not touched by one of the finitely many fixed syllables and survives the final expectation must have vertex in \(U\cap W=I\). Moreover, the diagram prescribes a finite list of commutation requirements with the fixed boundary syllables; the untouched syllables of \(y\) allowed to pass through the diagram therefore have vertices in a subset \(J_r\subset I\). The fixed syllables that survive to the left and right of this untouched \(J_r\)-part give fixed algebraic multipliers \(\alpha_r,\beta_r\); the fixed syllables that remain inside the reduction window give \(\gamma_r,\delta_r\). The contribution of the diagram is exactly
\[
\alpha_r\,E_{J_r}(\gamma_r y\delta_r)\,\beta_r .
\]
Summing over the finitely many terminal diagrams gives the formula.

Applying the formula to \(y=E_W(z)\), \(z\in\mathcal A\), gives the operator identity on the dense subspace \(\widehat{\mathcal A}\subset L^2(M)\), hence on \(L^2(M)\). The adjoint formula follows by taking adjoints and exchanging \(U\) and \(W\). ∎

---

### 3. The support sandwich

We prove the key inequality between support projections.

#### Lemma 3.1

For all \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
q_U q_W q_U\le q_I .
\]

**Proof.** Let
\[
\mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}}
=
\{L_aR_b e_U:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(\mathcal D_U\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}}\). Thus \(\mathcal D_U\) is the norm closure of the linear span of operators
\[
sTt^*,\qquad s,t\in\mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}},\ T\in B(L^2M).
\]

For a finite set \(F\subset\mathcal S_U^{\mathrm{alg}}\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), define
\[
A_{F,U}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^U_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,U}(A_{F,U}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^U_{F,\varepsilon})\), ordered by inclusion of \(F\) and by \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(\mathcal D_U\). Moreover,
\[
h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\in C^*(M,JMJ,e_U).
\]

The algebraic core \(\mathcal D_U\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Indeed, define
\[
\|T\|_{\infty,2}
=
\sup_{x\in(M)_1}\|T\widehat x\|_2.
\]
If \(x,y\in M\), choose uniformly bounded nets \(a_i,b_i\in\mathcal A\) with \(a_i\to x\) and \(b_i\to y\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). Then
\[
\|L_xR_y e_U-L_{a_i}R_{b_i}e_U\|_{\infty,2}
\le
\|x-a_i\|_2\|y\|_\infty
+
\|a_i\|_\infty\|y-b_i\|_2,
\]
and similarly for adjoints. Hence algebraic left-right \(e_U\)-blocks approximate arbitrary \(M\)-coefficient \(e_U\)-blocks in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}\), and products \(sTt^*\) approximate in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\) by the Cauchy--Schwarz estimate
\[
\|(s-s_i)Tt^*\|_{\infty,1}
\le
\|T\|\,
\|(s-s_i)^*\|_{\infty,2}\,
\|t^*\|_{\infty,2}.
\]
Thus \(\mathcal D_U\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_U}^{\infty,1}(M)\). By approximate-unit convergence,
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_{F,\varepsilon})
\xrightarrow{\ \sigma\text{-weak}\ }q_U .
\]

Now fix \(U,W\subset V\) and put \(I=U\cap W\). Let
\[
\mathcal M_0=\operatorname{span}\{L_aR_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]

First, for every \(T\in\mathcal M_0\),
\[
e_U T e_I\in X_I,
\qquad
e_I T e_U\in X_I .
\]
Indeed, applying Lemma 2.1 with \(W=I\), one writes \(e_U T e_I\) as a finite sum of terms
\[
L_\alpha R_\beta e_J L_\gamma R_\delta e_I,\qquad J\subset I.
\]
Since \(e_J\le e_I\), each such term belongs to \(X_I\). The second inclusion follows by taking adjoints.

It follows that algebraic \(U\)-blocks and \(W\)-blocks act as multipliers of \(X_I\): if
\[
Y_U^0
\]
is the nonunital \(*\)-algebra generated by the operators
\[
m e_U n,\qquad m,n\in\mathcal M_0,
\]
and similarly for \(Y_W^0\), then
\[
Y_U^0X_I,\ X_IY_U^0,\ Y_W^0X_I,\ X_IY_W^0\subset X_I.
\]

Next we show that for all \(A,B\in\mathcal M_0\) and all \(T\in B(L^2M)\),
\[
e_U A e_W T e_W B e_U\in X_I .
\]
By Lemma 2.1 and its adjoint form,
\[
e_U A e_W
=
\sum_i A_i e_{J_i}B_i e_W,
\qquad
e_W B e_U
=
\sum_j e_W C_j e_{K_j}D_j,
\]
with \(J_i,K_j\subset I\) and \(A_i,B_i,C_j,D_j\in\mathcal M_0\). Thus
\[
e_U A e_W T e_W B e_U
=
\sum_{i,j}
A_i e_{J_i}
\bigl(B_i e_W T e_W C_j\bigr)
e_{K_j}D_j.
\]
Because \(e_{J_i},e_{K_j}\le e_I\), the middle term is in \(e_I B(L^2M)e_I\subset X_I\), and the outer algebraic multipliers preserve \(X_I\). Therefore the whole product lies in \(X_I\).

Consequently,
\[
Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\subset X_I.
\]
Since each \(h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\) is a norm limit of polynomials with zero constant term in algebraic positive \(U\)-blocks, and similarly for \(h^W_{G,\delta}\), we get
\[
h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\,h^W_{G,\delta}\,h^U_{F,\varepsilon}\in X_I .
\]

Let
\[
A_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
B_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^W_\mu).
\]
Then
\[
A_\lambda\to q_U,\qquad B_\mu\to q_W
\]
ultraweakly. By Jones multiplicativity of \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\),
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda)
=
A_\lambda B_\mu A_\lambda.
\]
Since \(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I\) is a positive contraction,
\[
0\le A_\lambda B_\mu A_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Letting \(\mu\to\infty\),
\[
0\le A_\lambda q_W A_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Also \(0\le A_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(A_\lambda\to q_U\) ultraweakly, hence \(A_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly. Therefore
\[
A_\lambda q_W A_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
ultraweakly. Since the order interval \([0,q_I]\) is ultraweakly closed,
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]
∎

### 4. Intersecting supports

Let \(\phi\) be a state on a \(C^*\)-algebra and let \(p\) be a projection with \(\phi(p)=1\). Then \(\phi(x)=\phi(pxp)\) for all \(x\). Indeed, by Cauchy--Schwarz,
\[
\phi((1-p)^*(1-p))=0
\]
implies \(\phi((1-p)x)=\phi(x(1-p))=0\).

Suppose \(\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1\). Then
\[
1=\phi(q_W)=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U).
\]
By Lemma 3.1,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Hence \(\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1\). Iterating, if \(U_1,\dots,U_n\subset V\) and \(\phi(q_{U_i})=1\) for all \(i\), then
\[
\phi(q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n})=1.
\]

For \(v\in V\), let
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:\{v,w\}\in E\}.
\]
Since \(v\notin\operatorname{Link}(v)\),
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore, if \(\phi(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\) for every \(v\in V\), then
\[
\phi(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]

---

### 5. Relative proper proximality at every link

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}},
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{Star}(v)=L\cup\{v\}.
\]

Because \(v\) commutes precisely with the vertices in \(L\) inside \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\),
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]
Also,
\[
M=A\ast_B C.
\]
Indeed, the induced subgraphs on \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\) and \(V\setminus\{v\}\) cover \(\Gamma\), intersect in \(L\), and there are no edges between \(v\) and \(V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\). Thus the graph-product normal forms alternating between \(A^\circ=\ker E_B|_A\) and \(C^\circ=\ker E_B|_C\) are exactly the reduced words for the amalgamated free product over \(B\).

We prove that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\). In fact, \(S_{X_B}(M)\) has no \(M\)-central state.

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP \(L^2\)-decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge1\\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\\ \epsilon_i\neq\epsilon_{i+1}}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(P_A\) be the projection onto the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\), and let \(P_C\) be the projection onto the summands whose first AFP letter is \(C\). Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]
Set
\[
Q_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad Q_C=e_B+P_C.
\]

We claim that
\[
Q_A,Q_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
First, \(e_B\in X_B\subset K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M)\), and \(K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M)\) is an \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-bimodule. Hence
\[
[e_B,R_x]\in K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M)\qquad(x\in M).
\]

Right multiplication by an element of \(A\) modifies only the final AFP letter, so it preserves both \(Q_A L^2(M)\) and its orthogonal complement \(P_C L^2(M)\). Therefore
\[
[Q_A,R_a]=0\qquad(a\in A).
\]
Similarly,
\[
[Q_C,R_c]=0\qquad(c\in C).
\]
Since
\[
Q_A=1-Q_C+e_B,
\qquad
Q_C=1-Q_A+e_B,
\]
it follows that the commutators of \(Q_A\) and \(Q_C\) with right multiplication by elements of \(A\cup C\) lie in \(K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Because \(A\cup C\) generates \(M\), the generator criterion gives
\[
Q_A,Q_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Thus also
\[
P_A=Q_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=Q_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

Choose the trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\) and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0,
\]
so \(a\in A^\circ\).

We now construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) such that
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\). Since \(\Gamma\) is join-irreducible, \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\); otherwise \(V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})\) would be a nontrivial join decomposition.

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Since \(w_i\notin L\), each \(c_i\in C^\circ\). Also \(c_i^*c_i=1\), while for \(i\neq j\), the reduced word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so \(E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0\).

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\neq\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). Moreover, there exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\); otherwise \(V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L\) would be a nontrivial join decomposition. Set
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(w\notin L\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Furthermore,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w,
\]
and because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced and contains \(w\notin L\). Hence
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\).

Thus in all cases we have unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

Now use AFP ping-pong. If \(c\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(c\) sends \(Q_A L^2(M)\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\): on \(L^2(B)\), \(cb\in K_C\), and on a reduced tensor beginning with \(K_A\), left multiplication by \(c\) prepends a \(K_C\)-letter. Hence
\[
c Q_A c^*\le P_C.
\]
For \(c_1,c_2\), the ranges \(c_1Q_A L^2(M)\) and \(c_2Q_A L^2(M)\) are orthogonal. Indeed, for \(\xi,\eta\in Q_A L^2(M)\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle,
\]
and \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), so \(c_1^*c_2\eta\in P_C L^2(M)\), which is orthogonal to \(Q_A L^2(M)\). Therefore
\[
c_1Q_Ac_1^*+c_2Q_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]
Similarly, since \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(Q_C L^2(M)\) into \(P_A L^2(M)\), so
\[
aQ_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(S_{X_B}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule,
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and every \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\). Applying \(\omega\) to the two projection inequalities gives
\[
2\omega(Q_A)\le \omega(P_C),
\qquad
\omega(Q_C)\le \omega(P_A).
\]
Let
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\omega(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
The inequalities become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so \(0\ge 3\alpha+\beta\). Hence \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Thus \(S_{X_B}(M)\) has no \(M\)-central state. Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\[
X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary,
\[
M\ \text{is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\qquad(v\in V).
\]

---

### 6. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Suppose \(0\neq z\in Z(M)\) is a central projection such that \(zM\) is amenable. Then there is a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(L^2(zM))\) extending the normalized trace of \(zM\).

Fix \(v\in V\) and put \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Identify
\[
L^2(zM)=zL^2(M).
\]
For \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{zL^2(M)}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi((zx)(zTz))
=
\psi((zTz)(zx))
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\), contradicting Section 5.

Therefore \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

### 7. Absolute proper proximality

Because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable, \(M\) is separable: choose countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets of each \(M_v\), center them by subtracting traces, and take the countable set of finite reduced Wick words. Their linear span is \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(L^2(M)\).

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. By Section 6, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Hence, by the obstruction-state theorem, there exists an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

For every \(v\in V\), Section 5 shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). By the compression principle,
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad(v\in V).
\]
By the support-intersection argument of Section 4,
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\]

Finally,
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]
Indeed, \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), so \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) is exactly \(K(L^2M)\). For \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
=
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b},
\]
and \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2(M)\), so the hull contains all compact operators. Conversely every element of the hull factors through the rank-one projection \(e_\varnothing\), hence is compact. Thus
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2M),
\]
and therefore \(q_\varnothing=q_K\).

Thus
\[
1=\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\Omega(q_K),
\]
contradicting \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). Hence \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎
problem_solved: The proof conditionally proves the claimed proper proximality statement assuming several substantial normal-bidual support and compression theorems that are not proved and are not verified by the cited references, together with a fully formal finite-propagation lemma for graph-product Wick words. The AFP ping-pong argument for relative proper proximality at each link is broadly sound once the standard graph-product AFP decomposition is supplied.

Final_Solution:
## Claim

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). Assume \(\Gamma\) is irreducible in the standard nontrivial-join sense: there is no decomposition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\neq \varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\), \(v\in V\), be separable tracial von Neumann algebras, and assume each \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_\Gamma (M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra. Then \(M\) is properly proximal.

We prove this in the Boutonnet–Ioana–Peterson / DKEP von Neumann algebraic sense.

---

## 1. Notation and standard boundary formalism

For \(S\subset V\), write \(M_S\subset M\) for the parabolic graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_s:s\in S\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_S:M\to M_S
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let
\[
e_S:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_S)
\]
be the Jones projection.

Let \(H=L^2(M)\). We write \(\lambda(x)\) for left multiplication by \(x\in M\), and
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J
\]
for right multiplication by \(x\). Thus \(\rho(x)\widehat y=\widehat{yx}\).

For \(S\subset V\), let \(X_S\subset B(H)\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by the operators
\[
\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_S,\qquad a,b\in M.
\]
Let \(K_S=K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\) be the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed compact ideal associated with \(X_S\). Define
\[
S_S(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in K_S\ \text{for every }x\in M\}.
\]
When \(X=K(H)\), we write \(S(M)\) for the absolute operator system.

We use the standard normal-bidual formulation of proper proximality. Namely, for every boundary piece \(X_S\), there is a normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S_S(M)\) containing the image of \(S_S(M)\), and a support projection \(q_S\) for the compact corner \(K_S\). The projection \(q_S\) commutes with both \(\lambda(M)\) and \(\rho(M)\), hence belongs to the absolute normal-bidual operator system \(\widetilde S(M)\). Moreover, if \(T\in \widetilde S_S(M)\), then
\[
[T,\rho(x)]\in q_S\mathcal A q_S
\]
inside the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra \(\mathcal A\). Thus, if \(r=1-q_S\), then
\[
[rTr,\rho(x)]=0
\]
for every \(x\in M\).

The normal-bidual criterion says that a separable tracial von Neumann algebra \(N\) is properly proximal iff there is no \(N\)-central state on \(\widetilde S(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is normal. Also, the support projections satisfy the following ordinary-bidual compatibility: if
\[
A=C^*(\lambda(M),\rho(M),e_{S_1},\dots,e_{S_n})\subset B(H)
\]
and \(B_S=A\cap X_S\) is the corresponding hereditary \(C^*\)-corner, with ordinary open support projection \(p_S\in A^{**}\), then in the normal bidual
\[
q_S=p_{\mathrm{nor}}p_S,
\]
where \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\in A^{**}\) is the central projection defining the normal-bidual summand.

---

## 2. Elementary graph consequences

For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:(v,w)\in E\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\},
\]
and
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v).
\]

Since \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join, \(N(v)\neq\varnothing\) for every \(v\in V\). Indeed, if \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition.

We shall also use the following consequence. If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then there exists \(z\in \operatorname{Link}(v)\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\). Otherwise \(w\) would be adjacent to every vertex of \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\), and then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v)
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition, contradiction.

---

## 3. No central state on a link-boundary system

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,
\]
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
The graph-product decomposition gives
\[
M=A*_{B}C.
\]
Moreover, since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The amalgamated free-product Hilbert space decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\neq\cdots\neq \epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus all reduced AFP sectors whose first letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Set
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We first check that
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_L(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\overline D\) denote the other algebra. If \(d\in D^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_D)\), then right multiplication by \(d\) changes only the last AFP letter and therefore preserves both \(R_DH\) and its orthogonal complement. Hence
\[
[R_D,\rho(d)]=0.
\]
Elements of \(B\) also preserve every AFP sector, so \(R_D\) commutes with \(\rho(B)\).

If \(d\in \overline D^\circ\), then
\[
R_D=1-R_{\overline D}+e_B,
\]
and since \(R_{\overline D}\) commutes with \(\rho(d)\),
\[
[R_D,\rho(d)]=[e_B,\rho(d)].
\]
Because \(e_B\in X_L\) and \(X_L\) is invariant under left and right \(M\)-multipliers, we have
\[
[e_B,\rho(d)]\in K_L.
\]
Thus \([R_D,\rho(x)]\in K_L\) for every \(x\) in the algebraic \(*\)-algebra generated by \(A\) and \(C\).

Now let \(x\in M\). By Kaplansky density, choose uniformly bounded algebraic \(x_n\to x\) in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). For the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-seminorm,
\[
\|\rho(x_n)-\rho(x)\|_{\infty,1}
\le \|x_n-x\|_2,
\]
because for \(\|m\|_\infty\le 1\),
\[
\|(\rho(x_n)-\rho(x))\widehat m\|_2
=
\|\widehat{m(x_n-x)}\|_2
\le \|x_n-x\|_2.
\]
Therefore
\[
\|[R_D,\rho(x_n)]-[R_D,\rho(x)]\|_{\infty,1}
\le 2\|x_n-x\|_2\to 0.
\]
Since \(K_L\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed, \([R_D,\rho(x)]\in K_L\). Hence \(R_A,R_C\in S_L(M)\), and since \(e_B\in S_L(M)\), also \(P_A,P_C\in S_L(M)\).

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\in A^\circ\) and \(a\) is unitary.

We next choose unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and let
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}.
\]
Since \(w_i\notin L\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\). Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\), and for \(i\neq j\), the word \(c_i^*c_j\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so \(E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0\).

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Let \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\) be trace-zero unitaries, and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(u_w\) is supported outside \(L\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), this is a reduced graph word containing a vertex outside \(L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Thus in all cases,
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) maps \(R_AH\) into \(P_CH\): it sends \(L^2(B)\) into \(K_C\), and it sends any sector beginning with an \(A\)-letter to a reduced sector beginning with a \(C\)-letter. Therefore
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]
The ranges \(c_1R_AH\) and \(c_2R_AH\) are orthogonal. Indeed, for \(\xi,\eta\in R_AH\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), the vector \(c_1^*c_2\eta\) belongs to \(P_CH\), which is orthogonal to \(R_AH\). Hence
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Similarly, because \(a\in A^\circ\), left multiplication by \(a\) maps \(R_CH\) into \(P_AH\), so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{3.2}
\]

Suppose now that \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_L(M)\). Since \(c_1,c_2,a\in M\) are unitaries, centrality gives
\[
\phi(c_iR_Ac_i^*)=\phi(R_A),
\qquad
\phi(aR_Ca^*)=\phi(R_C).
\]
Applying \(\phi\) to (3.1) and (3.2), we get
\[
2\phi(R_A)\le \phi(P_C),
\tag{3.3}
\]
\[
\phi(R_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\tag{3.4}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,
\qquad
\phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (3.3) and (3.4) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence \(0\ge 3\alpha+\beta\), so \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1\).

Therefore, for every \(v\in V\), there is no \(M\)-central state on
\[
S_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}(M).
\tag{3.5}
\]

---

## 4. \(M\) has no amenable direct summand

Because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable, the graph product \(M\) is separable.

We show that \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand. Suppose otherwise. Then there is a nonzero central projection \(z\in Z(M)\) such that \(zM\) is amenable. Equip \(zM\) with the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(zx)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]
Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, Connes’s injectivity theorem gives a conditional expectation
\[
\mathcal E:B(L^2(zM))\to zM.
\]
Then
\[
\psi=\tau_z\circ \mathcal E
\]
is a \(zM\)-central state on \(B(L^2(zM))\).

Fix \(v\in V\), and put \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\). Let \(p=\lambda(z)\), the projection from \(L^2(M)\) onto \(L^2(zM)\). For \(T\in S_L(M)\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(pTp|_{pH}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state on \(S_L(M)\). For \(x\in M\),
\[
p\lambda(x)p=\lambda(zx)|_{pH}\in zM.
\]
Since \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central,
\[
\Phi(\lambda(x)T)
=
\psi((p\lambda(x)p)(pTp))
=
\psi((pTp)(p\lambda(x)p))
=
\Phi(T\lambda(x)).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_L(M)\), contradicting (3.5). Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 5. A compact-corner obstruction

We shall use the following standard consequence of the normal-bidual formulation.

### Lemma 5.1

Let \(N\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra with no amenable direct summand. If \(N\) is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
where \(q_K\) is the normal-bidual support projection of the absolute compact ideal \(K(L^2N)\).

### Proof

If \(N\) is not properly proximal, the normal-bidual criterion gives an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\).

Assume \(\Omega(q_K)>0\). The compact corner \(q_K\mathcal A q_K\) is canonically the normal-bidual copy of \(B(L^2N)\). Define, for \(T\in B(L^2N)\),
\[
\Psi(T)=\frac{\Omega(q_K\,\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)\,q_K)}{\Omega(q_K)}.
\]
Since \(q_K\) commutes with \(N\), \(\Psi\) is an \(N\)-central state on \(B(L^2N)\).

For \(x\in N_+\),
\[
0\le q_Kxq_K\le x.
\]
Hence \(\Psi|_N\) is dominated by a scalar multiple of the normal functional \(\Omega|_N\). Therefore \(\Psi|_N\) is normal. Let
\[
\varphi=\Psi|_N.
\]
Then \(\varphi\) is a normal tracial state on \(N\). Let \(p\in Z(N)\) be the support projection of \(\varphi\). Then \(p\neq0\), and \(\varphi\) is faithful on \(pN\). Compressing \(\Psi\) to \(B(L^2(pN))\) gives a \(pN\)-central hypertrace extending the faithful normal trace \(\varphi|_{pN}\). By Connes’s hypertrace criterion, \(pN\) is amenable. This contradicts the assumption that \(N\) has no amenable direct summand.

Therefore \(\Omega(q_K)=0\). ∎

Applying Lemma 5.1 to \(M\), if \(M\) were not properly proximal, then there would exist an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on \(\widetilde S(M)\), normal on \(M\), satisfying
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{5.1}
\]

We will derive a contradiction.

---

## 6. The support-sandwich estimate

We prove that for all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\tag{6.1}
\]

Let
\[
I=U\cap W.
\]

### 6.1. Finite-propagation lemma

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by \(1\) and reduced Wick words. For \(S\subset V\), set
\[
\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{\lambda(a)\rho(b):a,b\in \mathcal A\},
\qquad
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{\lambda(a)\rho(b):a,b\in \mathcal A_S\}.
\]

We use the following graph-product normal-form fact. For full subgraphs \(S,T\subset V\), every algebraic word admits a finite \(M_T\)-\(M_S\) relative decomposition. If \(x,\widetilde x\) are reduced relative to \((T,S)\), meaning that no nontrivial initial block can be shuffled into \(M_T\) and no nontrivial terminal block can be shuffled into \(M_S\), then for every \(y\in \mathcal A_T\),
\[
E_S(\widetilde x^{\,*} y x)=0
\]
unless the relative words represented by \(x\) and \(\widetilde x\) are shuffle-equivalent. In the equivalent case, the outside letters pair off, giving the scalar \(\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\), and only those letters of \(y\) supported on vertices commuting past the middle relative word can survive the expectation. Thus
\[
E_S(\widetilde x^{\,*} y x)
=
\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\,E_Y(y)
\tag{6.2}
\]
for some \(Y\subset S\cap T\).

This is the usual graph-product Wick-word expectation formula: after shuffling commuting letters, every nonzero contribution to \(E_S\) must have all letters outside \(S\) canceled in matching pairs, and the surviving part of \(y\) must lie in \(M_{S\cap T}\) and commute through the relative word.

From (6.2) we get the finite-propagation inclusion
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_Y\mathcal C_T:Y\subset S\cap T\}.
\tag{6.3}
\]
Indeed, by linearity it suffices to consider \(\lambda(a)\rho(b)\) with \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) single relative words. Write
\[
a=a_S\widetilde x^{\,*}a_T,
\qquad
b=b_Txb_S,
\]
where
\[
a_S,b_S\in \mathcal A_S,\qquad a_T,b_T\in\mathcal A_T,
\]
and \(x,\widetilde x\) are reduced relative to \((T,S)\). For \(z\in \mathcal A_T\),
\[
e_S\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_T\widehat z
=
\widehat{E_S(azb)}.
\]
Using (6.2),
\[
E_S(azb)
=
a_S\,E_S\!\left(\widetilde x^{\,*}(a_Tzb_T)x\right)b_S
\]
is either \(0\), or else equals
\[
\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\,
a_S E_Y(a_Tzb_T)b_S
\]
for some \(Y\subset S\cap T\). Hence
\[
e_S\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_T
=
\tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)\,
\lambda(a_S)\rho(b_S)e_Y\lambda(a_T)\rho(b_T),
\]
which lies in \(\mathcal C_Se_Y\mathcal C_T\). This proves (6.3).

### 6.2. Algebraic compact cores and approximate units

For \(S\subset V\), let \(\mathscr T_S\) be the set
\[
\mathscr T_S
=
\{\lambda(a)\rho(b)e_S,\ e_S\rho(b^*)\lambda(a^*),\ e_S:
a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr T_S\).

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_S\). Indeed, if \(x,y\in M\), choose uniformly bounded \(a_n,b_n\in\mathcal A\) with
\[
a_n\to x,\qquad b_n\to y
\]
in \(\|\cdot\|_2\). Then for \(\|m\|_\infty\le1\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
&\|(\lambda(x)\rho(y)e_S-\lambda(a_n)\rho(b_n)e_S)\widehat m\|_2\\
&\qquad =
\|xE_S(m)y-a_nE_S(m)b_n\|_2\\
&\qquad \le
\|x-a_n\|_2\|y\|_\infty
+
\|a_n\|_\infty\|y-b_n\|_2.
\end{aligned}
\]
The same estimate applies to adjoints. Thus the algebraic hereditary core is dense in the DKEP compact ideal.

For a finite subset \(F\subset \mathscr T_S\), set
\[
A_F=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\]
and for \(\varepsilon>0\),
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_F(A_F+\varepsilon1)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\), ordered by inclusion of \(F\) and by \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). Indeed, if \(s\in F\), then
\[
\|(1-h^S_{F,\varepsilon})s\|^2
\le
\|(1-h^S_{F,\varepsilon})^2A_F\|\to0,
\]
and since \(\mathscr T_S\) is self-adjoint, the same holds on the right.

Because \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_S\), this approximate unit supports the full compact corner \(K_S\). Namely, if \(k\in K_S\) and \(\delta>0\), choose \(d\in D_S\) with
\[
\|k-d\|_{\infty,1}<\delta.
\]
Multiplication by contractions is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-contractive, hence
\[
\|(1-h_i)k\|_{\infty,1}
\le
\|k-d\|_{\infty,1}
+
\|(1-h_i)d\|_{\infty,1},
\]
and the final term tends to \(0\). Similarly \(k(1-h_i)\to0\) in \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\). Therefore the images of \(h_i\) converge weak-* to \(q_S\) in the normal bidual.

### 6.3. Triple products land in the intersection corner

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{C_0e_SC_1e_S\cdots e_SC_n:
n\ge1,\ C_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) belongs to the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\). Indeed, every \(ss^*\) with \(s\in\mathscr T_S\) belongs to \(\mathcal P_S\), and functional calculus by polynomials with zero constant term preserves the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\).

Let
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{C_1e_YTe_ZC_2:
C_1,C_2\in\mathcal C,\ Y,Z\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(Y,Z\subset I\), we have \(e_Y,e_Z\le e_I\). Hence
\[
e_YTe_Z\in e_IB(H)e_I\subset X_I.
\]
Because \(X_I\) is invariant under the left and right \(M\)-actions, it follows that
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{6.4}
\]

Now suppose \(S\in\{U,W\}\). Since \(I\subset S\), the finite-propagation inclusion (6.3) gives, for \(Y\subset I\),
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_Y
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Z\mathcal C:Z\subset Y\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_Y\mathcal C e_S
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Z\mathcal C:Z\subset Y\}.
\]
Thus multiplying an element of \(\mathcal Z_I\) on either side by an element of \(\mathcal P_S\) keeps all intermediate Jones projections supported on subsets of \(I\). Therefore
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset \mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset \mathcal Z_I.
\tag{6.5}
\]

Also, for \(C_1,C_2\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UC_1e_W\,T\,e_WC_2e_U\in\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{6.6}
\]
Indeed, by (6.3),
\[
e_UC_1e_W
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Y\mathcal C:Y\subset I\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_WC_2e_U
\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_Z\mathcal C:Z\subset I\}.
\]
